why: This is the composition root and process entrypoint. It wires the whole app together in order: logger, DB, matching engine, DI container, HTTP router, WebSocket server, order-book recovery from the DB, and all schedulers, then blocks on the HTTP listener. Without it there is no runnable binary and nothing decides the startup sequence (e.g. recovering books before serving traffic).

key_pieces.name: main
key_pieces.note: Fixes the exact boot order — engine and container before router, and recoverOrderBook before router.Run so open orders are back in memory before requests arrive.

why: Builds the Gin engine, instantiates every controller from the container, and declares the full REST surface split into public, JWT-authed, and admin route groups with their middleware. It exists to centralize routing and auth boundaries in one place. Without it no HTTP endpoint would be reachable and the public/private/admin access split would be undefined.

key_pieces.name: setupRoutes
key_pieces.note: The single source of truth for which endpoints are public vs. require AuthMiddleware vs. AdminMiddleware.

why: Opens the embedded SQLite database with the specific pragmas (WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, busy_timeout, single connection) that lift order throughput ~10x, and ensures/migrates the ohlcv_1min and credentials tables plus seeds the in-memory counters. It exists because the DB tuning and idempotent schema fixes are load-bearing correctness/perf decisions, not incidental. Without it the app would fsync per commit (~60 orders/sec), lose sessions on restart, and render broken charts.

key_pieces.name: initDB
key_pieces.note: The single-writer + WAL choice is deliberate; the comment explains a connection pool measured slower for SQLite writes.

key_pieces.name: seedSystemCounters
key_pieces.note: Loads historical order/trade totals so the live UI badge reflects all history, not just this process's uptime.

why: Holds the boot-time helper functions main.go calls: log file setup, running SQL schema/seed files in a transaction, recovering the order book from persisted open orders, starting all schedulers, and mounting the WebSocket server on :8081. It exists to keep main.go thin and group one-time startup mechanics. Without it there is no crash recovery of open orders and no wiring of the WS listener.

key_pieces.name: recoverOrderBook
key_pieces.note: Reloads NEW/PARTIAL orders per market into the engine at boot so a restart doesn't lose the live book.

key_pieces.name: executeSQLFileWithTransaction
key_pieces.note: Runs seed/schema files atomically, splitting on semicolons — only used in testMode.

why: HTTP handlers for the admin group: manual balance settlement, a stubbed make-market, and the full exchange reset. It exists to expose privileged operational actions behind the admin middleware, separate from user-facing controllers. Without it there would be no way to top up a demo user or wipe/re-seed the exchange between demo runs.

key_pieces.name: AdminController.Reset
key_pieces.note: Entry point for the live wipe-and-refund used to reset the demo without a restart.

why: Single HTTP handler that reads the authenticated userId from context and returns that user's balances with USDT valuations. It exists as the thin transport layer over IBalanceService. Without it the authenticated /balances endpoint would have no handler.

key_pieces.name: BalanceController.GetBalances
key_pieces.note: Relies on MustGet("userId") populated by AuthMiddleware, so it never trusts a client-supplied user id.

why: HTTP handlers for public market data: all-markets snapshot, single-market data, and OHLCV history with query-param parsing, defaulting, and bounds checking (limit ≤ 1000, default 6-month window). It exists to validate and translate untrusted query strings into a typed GetOhlcvDataReq before hitting the service. Without it the chart and ticker endpoints would be unserved and unvalidated.

key_pieces.name: MarketDataController.GetOHLCVHistory
key_pieces.note: Does all the time-range/limit sanitization and validates the interval against SupportedIntervals.

why: One public handler returning the top-of-book snapshot for a given market. It exists as the transport shim over IOrderBookService.GetSnapshot. Without it clients couldn't fetch the initial order-book depth before subscribing over WebSocket.

Authenticated handlers for placing, cancelling, and paginating orders. It pulls the user/userId from context, binds the JSON/query request, and delegates to IOrderService, mapping errors to coded responses. It exists because order placement is the core write path and needs careful extraction of the authenticated principal. Without it users couldn't trade or view their order history.

key_pieces.name: OrderController.PlaceOrder
key_pieces.note: Reads the full *dto.User (for fee tiers) from context rather than just an id, unlike the other handlers.

why: Defines the uniform API envelope (Resp with code/message/timestamp/data) and the full catalogue of string MessageCodes plus helper constructors for success/error responses. It exists so every endpoint returns a consistent shape and a stable error-code taxonomy. Without it each handler would hand-roll response JSON and error codes would be ad hoc.

key_pieces.name: MessageCode
key_pieces.note: Namespaced string codes (users 2xxxxxx, orders 3xxxxxx, etc.) give clients a stable contract independent of HTTP status.

why: Handlers for register, login, get-profile, and logout, binding request DTOs and delegating to IUserService. It exists as the auth/identity transport layer. Without it there'd be no way to create accounts or obtain the login token every private endpoint requires.

The heart of the candlestick subsystem: subscribes to the trade stream, batches trades, fans them out per-symbol to a worker pool, runs per-interval timers that close bars on boundaries, and periodically flushes realtime bars to the repo. It exists to turn a raw trade firehose into time-bucketed OHLCV data concurrently and durably. Without it there would be no live or historical candles.

key_pieces.name: OHLCVAggregator.aggregateTrades
key_pieces.note: Batches by size or flush-interval before processing, which is what makes high trade rates tractable.

key_pieces.name: OHLCVAggregator.startIntervalTimer
key_pieces.note: Per-interval goroutine that detects a crossed bucket boundary and persists the just-closed bar.

why: Declares the supported intervals→(duration, table) map and the aggregator tuning knobs (batch size, flush interval, channel size, concurrency) with validation and defaults. It exists to centralize OHLCV configuration and guard against nonsensical values. Without it interval-to-table mapping and safe aggregator defaults would be scattered or missing.

key_pieces.name: SupportedIntervals
key_pieces.note: The authoritative list of which candle intervals exist and which SQLite table each writes to.

why: Defines the TradeStream and OHLCVRepository interfaces that decouple the aggregator from concrete stream and storage implementations. It exists so the aggregator can be tested and swapped (e.g. SQLite vs. another store) without code changes. Without it the aggregator would be hard-wired to concrete types.

Defines the OHLCV domain types: the interval constants, the incoming Trade, the API response shape OHLCV, and the internal mutable OHLCVBar with its update logic. It exists to hold the value objects and the core per-trade bar math (high/low/close/volume accumulation). Without it there'd be no representation of a candle or the rule for folding a trade into one.

key_pieces.name: OHLCVBar.Update
key_pieces.note: The actual OHLCV accumulation rule — max high, min low, last close, summed volume/quote-volume.

why: The SQLite implementation of OHLCVRepository: reads closed bars for the API, upserts/accumulates closed bars per (symbol, open_time), and manages the realtime bar table. It exists to persist candles durably and encode the merge rule when a bar already exists. Without it candles would live only in memory and vanish on restart.

key_pieces.name: SQLiteOHLCVRepository.UpsertOHLCVBars
key_pieces.note: Encodes the accumulate-on-conflict rule (keep open, max high, min low, update close, sum volume) inside a transaction.

key_pieces.name: SQLiteOHLCVRepository.getTableName
key_pieces.note: Maps interval to its dedicated table, the mechanism behind per-interval table sharding.

why: Holds one symbol's live bars across all intervals in a mutex-guarded map and applies trades to the correct time bucket, plus closes bars and seeds the next one. It exists to isolate per-symbol concurrent state so different symbols never contend. Without it trades couldn't be safely bucketed per interval in memory.

key_pieces.name: RealtimeSymbolBars.UpdateByTrades
key_pieces.note: Groups a trade batch by (interval, bucket) and folds each group into the right bar under one lock.

key_pieces.name: RealtimeSymbolBars.CloseBars
key_pieces.note: Closes elapsed buckets and rolls a fresh bar seeded at the previous close price for continuity.

why: A minimal in-process TradeStream backed by a buffered channel, letting the order service push trades and the aggregator consume them. It exists as the concrete, single-node implementation of the stream interface. Without it there'd be no pipe connecting trade settlement to candle aggregation.

key_pieces.name: SimpleTradeStream.SyncTrade
key_pieces.note: The producer side the order service calls after each match to feed the candle pipeline.

why: Time-bucket helpers that truncate a timestamp to its interval boundary and compute the next bucket. It exists so bucket alignment logic is defined once and reused by the aggregator and symbol bars. Without it every caller would re-derive candle boundary math and risk inconsistency.

key_pieces.name: getBucketUnixTime
key_pieces.note: The single definition of how a trade timestamp maps to a candle's open time.

why: A generic bounded worker pool (fixed goroutines consuming a task channel) used by the aggregator to process symbols concurrently. It exists to cap concurrency and reuse goroutines rather than spawning one per batch. Without it per-symbol bar updates would either serialize or spawn unbounded goroutines.

key_pieces.name: WorkerPool.Submit
key_pieces.note: Non-blocking submit that drops the task if the queue is full, trading a lost update for never blocking the aggregator.

why: The API-facing balance struct carrying available/locked/total plus a USDT valuation. It exists to shape balance responses for clients, distinct from any DB row. Without it the balance service would have no serializable output type with valuation fields.
The market-ticker DTO: name, latest price, 24h change, 24h volume. It exists as the cached/served shape for market data. Without it the market-data cache and endpoints would lack a value type.

The central order DTO with custom JSON marshalling, a fluent OrderBuilder, conversion to the engine's order type, and the PlaceOrderContext/AssetDetails structs that thread state through placement. It exists as the boundary object between API, engine, and persistence, hiding internal fields and formatting fees/timestamps for clients. Without it order data couldn't move cleanly across layers or serialize correctly.

key_pieces.name: Order.MarshalJSON
key_pieces.note: Omits price for market orders and formats fee rate/timestamps, decoupling wire format from storage.

key_pieces.name: OrderBuilder
key_pieces.note: Fluent construction that guarantees an id/timestamps/NEW status, used by the service helpers.

key_pieces.name: PlaceOrderContext
key_pieces.note: Carries market, user, request, fee info, assets, and resulting trades through the multi-phase placement flow.

why: Declares all inbound request bodies/queries (register, login, settlement, order, orders-query) with Gin binding tags encoding the validation rules. It exists to centralize request contracts and let Gin reject malformed input before it reaches handlers. Without it there'd be no typed, validated request parsing.

key_pieces.name: OrderReq
key_pieces.note: Binding tags encode the trading rules (side/type/mode enums, mode required only for limit) at the edge.

why: A generic pagination envelope used for list responses. It exists to give every paginated endpoint a consistent shape with total/pages/has-next metadata. Without it each list endpoint would reinvent paging fields.

key_pieces.name: PaginationResp
key_pieces.note: Go-generic over the element type, so orders and any future list share one response shape.

why: Response types for a placed order's result and its individual matches, with custom JSON to emit epoch-millis timestamps. It exists to shape the PlaceOrder response distinct from the internal engine trade. Without it clients wouldn't get a clean matches array after placing an order.

The user DTO with password hash hidden from JSON and a custom marshaller emitting created_at as epoch millis. It exists as the safe, serializable representation of a user across layers. Without it user responses could leak the hash or use inconsistent time formats.

key_pieces.name: User.MarshalJSON
key_pieces.note: Guarantees the password hash is never serialized and normalizes the timestamp.

why: Represents one side (bid/ask) of a book as a price-ordered treemap of FIFO deques, tracking total volume and quote amount. It exists to give O(log n) best-price access and O(1) order append/pop per price level. Without it the matching engine couldn't efficiently find and consume the best price.

key_pieces.name: BookSide.BestPrice
key_pieces.note: Encodes the side-dependent priority — max price for bids, min for asks.

key_pieces.name: BookSide.PutToHead
key_pieces.note: Returns a partially-filled counter-order to the front of its level to preserve time priority.

why: A map from order id to its side/price/node for O(1) lookup. It exists so cancels and fills can locate an order without scanning price levels. Without it every cancel would be a linear search across the book.

key_pieces.name: OrderIndex.Get
key_pieces.note: Returns the side, price, and node needed to remove an order from the correct level in O(1).

why: The full order book for one market: the matching algorithm for limit/market taker and maker orders, cancel, latest-price tracking, and the top-20 snapshot, all under RW locks. It exists as the core matching state machine per market. Without it there is no price-time-priority matching — the exchange's central function.

key_pieces.name: OrderBook.PlaceOrder
key_pieces.note: Dispatches on order type/mode into maker-add vs. taker-match flows — the matching entrypoint.

key_pieces.name: OrderBook.takeMarketBidOrder
key_pieces.note: Special-cases quote-amount-driven market buys (consume until quote spent), the trickiest path.

key_pieces.name: OrderBook.RefreshSnapshot
key_pieces.note: Precomputes the top-20 depth so read APIs don't lock the live book on every request.

why: The MatchingEngine facade owning one order book per market and routing place/cancel/snapshot/recover calls to the right book. It exists as the single entry point services use, hiding per-market book management. Without it callers would juggle a map of books and there'd be no unified reset/recover.

key_pieces.name: MatchingEngine.RecoverOrderBook
key_pieces.note: Rehydrates open orders into a market's book at startup and sets latest price.

key_pieces.name: MatchingEngine.Reset
key_pieces.note: Recreates all books empty — the in-memory half of the admin exchange reset.

why: Defines MarketInfo (name/base/quote) and a manager for looking up markets and their asset pairs. It exists as the lightweight metadata layer describing what pairs exist. Without it the engine and services couldn't resolve a market string into its base/quote assets.

The engine's internal order representation plus side/mode/type/status enums, the status-derivation rule, and the OrderNode linked-list element. It exists as the pure matching-domain model, deliberately lighter than the persistence DTO. Without it the book and deque would have no element type and status transitions would be undefined.

key_pieces.name: Order.GetStatus
key_pieces.note: Derives NEW/PARTIAL/FILLED from remaining vs. original size using the utils.Scale epsilon.

key_pieces.name: OrderNode
key_pieces.note: Embeds prev/next pointers so an order can live directly in the intrusive deque.

why: An intrusive doubly-linked-list deque of OrderNodes giving O(1) push/pop/remove and a running volume. It exists to hold all orders at ONE price level in first-in-first-out order — which is exactly price-time priority: at the same price, whoever arrived earlier is filled first. Each method maps to a specific order-book action (rest a maker, fill a taker, cancel). Without it a price level couldn't queue orders fairly with fast arbitrary removal. Note it tracks two different numbers: size = count of orders, volume = sum of their remaining quantities.

key_pieces.name: PushBack
key_pieces.note: Rests a NEW maker order at the BACK of the queue (newest last). Back-insertion is what enforces time priority — earlier orders sit nearer the front and fill first. O(1).

key_pieces.name: PushHead
key_pieces.note: Inserts at the FRONT. Used to return a partially-filled order to the head so it KEEPS its original early time priority instead of losing its place to the back.

key_pieces.name: PopFront
key_pieces.note: Removes and returns the OLDEST order. Matching eats liquidity front-to-back, so PopFront is how a fully-filled maker leaves the level. O(1).

key_pieces.name: PeekFront
key_pieces.note: Looks at the oldest order WITHOUT removing it — the matcher peeks to test the price-cross and compute the fill before deciding to pop it or just reduce its remaining size.

key_pieces.name: Remove
key_pieces.note: O(1) delete of ANY node (head, tail, or middle) via its embedded prev/next pointers — this is what makes cancelling an order cheap, with no scan of the queue.

key_pieces.name: IsEmpty
key_pieces.note: Lets the book side detect an exhausted price level so it can drop the whole level from the price tree.

key_pieces.name: Size
key_pieces.note: O(1) count of orders at this level (kept as a running counter, never recomputed).

key_pieces.name: Volume
key_pieces.note: O(1) total remaining quantity resting at this level — the number shown as market depth and consumed during matching.

key_pieces.name: QuoteAmount
key_pieces.note: price × volume = the notional value (in quote currency) resting at this level, used by market-buy-by-quote-amount math.

why: A tiny typed cache-key prefix helper that namespaces keys, with the market-data prefix constant. It exists to avoid raw string key collisions in the shared cache and to guarantee the cache writer (market job) and readers (market service) build the exact same key. Without it cache keys would be unprefixed strings prone to clashes or silent writer/reader mismatches (a permanent cache miss).

key_pieces.name: CacheKeyPrefix.Apply
key_pieces.note: The one canonical way to build a namespaced key (MDC:BTC-USDT), used identically by the cache's writer and readers.

why: Central static config: the supported asset list, the full market list the engine boots, AMM per-level quote caps, and the reserved margin/AMM account ids. It exists as the single place defining what the exchange trades and the special internal accounts. Without it the engine, AMM, and fee settlement would have no canonical market/account definitions.

key_pieces.name: ALL_MARKETS
key_pieces.note: The authoritative market list every subsystem iterates (engine, schedulers, metrics, aggregator).

key_pieces.name: MARGIN_ACCOUNT_ID / INTERNAL_AMM_ACCOUNT_ID
key_pieces.note: Reserved account ids for fee revenue and the market-making bot.

why: Ethereum helper for balance checks and native ETH transfers via go-ethereum, with ERC-20 transfer left as a TODO. It exists as a scaffold for on-chain deposit/withdrawal integration. Its purpose is only partially realized — ERC-20 transfer and check are stubs and it isn't referenced from the boot path, so it currently reads as forward-looking/experimental rather than load-bearing.

key_pieces.name: CheckBalance
key_pieces.note: Only the ETH branch is implemented; ERC-20 returns true unconditionally — a placeholder.

why: Represents one WebSocket connection: read pump (parse subscribe/unsubscribe), write pump (send + periodic ping with deadlines), and the upgrade handler that spawns both. It exists to manage per-connection lifecycle and keep-alive so clients receive live pushes reliably. Without it there'd be no way to accept and service a WebSocket client.

key_pieces.name: Client.WritePump
key_pieces.note: Adds ping/deadline keep-alive so dead connections are detected and cleaned up.

key_pieces.name: HandleWebSocket
key_pieces.note: Upgrades the HTTP request and launches the read/write goroutines per client.

why: The central WebSocket hub: tracks clients and per-subscription-key client sets, and handles register/unregister/subscribe/broadcast under a mutex. It exists as the fan-out point so the feeder can push a payload to exactly the clients subscribed to a channel. Without it there'd be no routing of live data to interested subscribers.

key_pieces.name: Hub.BroadcastToSubscribers
key_pieces.note: Sends only to clients subscribed to a key and drops slow clients rather than blocking.

key_pieces.name: Hub.Run
key_pieces.note: Serializes all client-set mutations through one goroutine via channels.

why: A demo data feeder that pushes fabricated OHLCV and order-book messages on tickers. It exists as a standalone test/demo aid for the WS front-end before real data is wired. It appears unused in the boot path (the real feeder is the scheduler) and one method is a TODO stub, so it's a development scaffold, not production wiring.

Defines the WebSocket protocol: channel constants, action types, request/response envelopes, per-channel param structs, and the BuildSubscriptionKey that normalizes a request into a routable key. It exists as the wire contract and the logic that turns params into a stable subscription identity. Without it the hub couldn't distinguish subscriptions or parse client messages.

key_pieces.name: BuildSubscriptionKey
key_pieces.note: Per-channel param decoding that produces the exact key the hub fans out on.

key_pieces.name: WSChannel constants
key_pieces.note: Enumerates the public and private channels (incl. per-user USER_DATA and public SYSSTATS).

why: The dependency-injection container that constructs and wires every repository, service, scheduler, cache, proxy, metrics, OHLCV aggregator, and WS hub in the correct order. It exists so the rest of the app depends on interfaces while one place owns concrete construction and lifecycle. Without it wiring would be scattered and the careful init ordering (repos→ohlcv→services→ws→proxy→schedulers→metrics) would be fragile.

key_pieces.name: Container.initOHLCVAgg
key_pieces.note: Seeds each symbol's first candle from a real index/last price with a sane fallback to avoid a chart-wrecking 0.01 bar.

key_pieces.name: NewContainer
key_pieces.note: Encodes the dependency ordering the whole system relies on.

why: Process-wide atomic counters for total orders/trades plus an inferred simulation-duration tracker, living in the leaf utils package to be shared without import cycles. It exists so the order service (writer) and WS feeder (reader) can share system-wide live stats. Without it the live dashboard badge and orders/sec rate would have no source.

key_pieces.name: SimTick
key_pieces.note: Infers whether a load sim is 'running' purely from order-flow deltas, needing no start/stop signal.

why: Float rounding helpers and the shared Scale epsilon (1e-7) used everywhere for money comparisons and fill/refund rounding. It exists to make float handling consistent across engine, settlement, and repos. Without it dust amounts and rounding would be handled inconsistently, risking conservation errors.

key_pieces.name: Scale
key_pieces.note: The one epsilon that defines 'effectively zero' for order status and balance math across the codebase.

why: A token→user cache backed by a credentials table, rehydrating from the DB on a miss so sessions survive restarts. It exists to make auth fast in-memory while durable across reboots. Without it every restart would invalidate all logins and each auth check would hit the DB.

key_pieces.name: CredentialCache.Get
key_pieces.note: On cache miss, reloads the token and user from SQLite — the mechanism that keeps sessions alive across restarts.

why: Implements the admin service: transactional exchange reset (wipe orders/trades, zero and re-fund demo balances, clear candles, reset engine and counters), manual settlement, and a scripted test market-maker. It exists to encode privileged multi-table operations atomically. Without it there'd be no safe way to reset the demo or manually adjust balances.

key_pieces.name: adminService.ResetExchange
key_pieces.note: Coordinates DB wipe + engine reset + counter reset so memory and DB start from an identical blank slate, without a process restart.

why: Implements balance retrieval, enriching each asset with a USDT valuation by looking up market data (USDT valued at face). It exists to combine raw balances with live prices for the portfolio view. Without it clients would get quantities but no total portfolio value.

key_pieces.name: balanceService.GetBalances
key_pieces.note: Joins balances to cached market prices to compute per-asset USDT valuation.

why: A trivial thread-safe in-memory key/value cache implementing ICacheService. It exists as the shared store the market-data scheduler writes and the market-data service reads. Without it computed market tickers couldn't be cached between the 30s scheduler runs and API reads.

Implements market data: serving cached tickers, computing latest price / 24h change / 24h volume from the trades table, and proxying OHLCV history to the aggregator. It exists to separate cheap cached reads from the expensive periodic calculation. Without it there'd be no market statistics or history endpoint backing.

key_pieces.name: MarketDataService.CalculateMarketData
key_pieces.note: The expensive computation the scheduler runs periodically and caches, not on the request path.

why: Thin service exposing order-book snapshot, latest price, and base/quote assets by delegating to the engine. It exists as the service-layer boundary so controllers/AMM don't touch the engine directly. Without it engine access would leak into higher layers.

A single WithTx helper that begins a transaction, runs a closure, and commits or rolls back (including on panic). It exists so every multi-step DB mutation shares one correct commit/rollback pattern. Without it each service would hand-roll transaction handling and risk leaked or half-committed transactions.

key_pieces.name: WithTx
key_pieces.note: Centralizes rollback-on-error and rollback-then-repanic, the safety net for all money-moving writes.

why: The core order service orchestrating placement (via limit/market strategies), the two-phase flow (freeze funds + insert + engine match + persist trades in one tx, then settle balances), cancellation with refund, and all order queries. It exists as the transactional coordinator between engine, balances, orders, trades, and the candle stream. Without it there'd be no correct, atomic path from a request to matched-and-settled state.

key_pieces.name: orderService.executeOrderPlacementPhase
key_pieces.note: The atomic Phase-1: lock funds, insert order, run the engine, persist trades, and feed the candle stream in one transaction.

key_pieces.name: orderService.CancelOrder
key_pieces.note: Validates ownership/status, cancels in the engine, then refunds the correct locked asset in a tx.

key_pieces.name: OrderPlacementStrategy
key_pieces.note: Strategy split cleanly separates limit vs. market-order construction and handling.

why: Implements registration (secure id gen, bcrypt hash, uniqueness check, balance provisioning with a 500 USDT starter), login (bcrypt verify + token issue), and logout. It exists to own identity/credential logic and initial account funding. Without it users couldn't be created with hashed passwords or seeded balances.

key_pieces.name: userService.Register
key_pieces.note: Creates user, provisions all-asset balances, and grants starter USDT in one transaction.

key_pieces.name: genUIDSecure
key_pieces.note: Generates the Binance-style dated alphanumeric UID using crypto/rand.

why: Registers Prometheus gauges (bid/ask volume, latest price, open-order count, scheduler run counts) and refreshes them from the book/order services and scheduler reporter. It exists to expose operational telemetry at /metrics. Without it there'd be no Prometheus-scrapable view of exchange state.

key_pieces.name: MetricService.UpdateMetrics
key_pieces.note: Pulled on each scrape by the router so gauges reflect current per-market state.

why: Defines the market-making strategy interface and a factory selecting a strategy by enum (only provide-liquidity implemented). It exists to leave room for multiple AMM strategies behind one interface. Without it the liquidity scheduler couldn't obtain a strategy polymorphically.

key_pieces.name: GetStrategy
key_pieces.note: Factory that currently returns only the provide-liquidity strategy; grid/arbitrage are declared but unimplemented.

why: The provide-liquidity market-maker: fetches index price, balance, and open orders, computes 20 ideal bid/ask levels around mid, and cancels/replaces orders to converge on them. It exists to keep the demo order books populated with two-sided liquidity so users can always trade. Without it the books would be empty absent real users and matching couldn't happen.

key_pieces.name: ProvideLiquidityStrategy.CalculateIdealPriceLevels
key_pieces.note: Derives the target ladder from index price, spread, and available balance per side.

key_pieces.name: ProvideLiquidityStrategy.AdjustOrdersForSide
key_pieces.note: The reconcile loop — cancels off-target orders and places missing levels, the core of quote maintenance.

why: The AMM's exchange-facade proxy plus its Balance value type: it adapts internal services (order book, balance, order, user) and the external BTSE index-price API into the interface the strategy consumes. It exists to give the strategy one clean surface instead of many service dependencies. Without it the strategy would be coupled directly to every service and the external price API.

key_pieces.name: AmmExchangeFuncProxyImpl.GetIndexPrice
key_pieces.note: Fetches BTSE market price with a fallback to last-traded price, so market-making has a mid even when the API is blocked.

key_pieces.name: IAmmExchangeFuncProxy
key_pieces.note: The narrow interface that lets the strategy be tested/decoupled from concrete services.

why: Pure helper functions for order placement: parse market to assets, decide which asset/amount to freeze, build order DTOs/engine orders, determine fee asset/rate, compute cancel refunds, and wrap the response. It exists to keep the order service lean by extracting stateless transformations. Without it that logic would bloat the service and be harder to reason about per order type.

key_pieces.name: DetermineFreezeValue
key_pieces.note: Encodes the correct locked asset/amount per side and order type — the pre-trade risk rule.

key_pieces.name: CalculateRefund
key_pieces.note: Determines the exact asset/amount to unlock when cancelling, mirroring the freeze logic.

why: The settlement engine that turns a set of matched trades into concrete order updates and per-user balance deltas, handling limit-buy overpayment refunds, fee deduction on both sides, and fee-revenue totals. It exists to centralize the delicate money-conservation math after matching. Without it fills wouldn't translate correctly into balances and fees, risking value leaks.

key_pieces.name: ProcessTradeSettlement
key_pieces.note: The top-level pass building all order updates and user settlements from a trade batch.

key_pieces.name: processBidUserBalances
key_pieces.note: Handles the limit-buy case of unlocking at order price and refunding the overpayment vs. actual fill price.

why: Declares all service-layer interfaces (user, order, order book, balance, admin, cache, market data, AMM). It exists so controllers, the container, and cross-service calls depend on abstractions, enabling substitution and testing. Without it the layers would be coupled to concrete implementations and DI would be rigid.

key_pieces.name: IOrderService
key_pieces.note: The broadest contract — placement, cancel, and the various query shapes the rest of the app relies on.

why: Defines the DBExecutor abstraction (so methods accept either *sql.DB or *sql.Tx) and all repository interfaces for users, balances, orders, and trades. It exists to let the same query methods run inside or outside a transaction and to keep SQL behind interfaces. Without it services couldn't compose repo calls into transactions cleanly.

key_pieces.name: DBExecutor
key_pieces.note: The key abstraction letting every repo method run under a shared transaction or a plain connection.

key_pieces.name: IBalanceRepository
key_pieces.note: Its documented lock/unlock/modify semantics are the contract the money path depends on.

why: SQLite balance repository implementing atomic available/locked mutations via conditional UPDATEs that fail (0 rows) when funds are insufficient. It exists to enforce balance invariants at the database level rather than in racy application checks. Without it concurrent orders could over-spend a balance.

key_pieces.name: balanceRepository.LockedByUserIdAndAsset
key_pieces.note: Single atomic UPDATE with an available >= ? guard — the concurrency-safe fund freeze.

key_pieces.name: balanceRepository.UpdateAsset
key_pieces.note: Applies signed available/locked deltas, the primitive settlement uses to move funds.

why: SQLite order repository with insert/update, several status/market filtered queries, an SQL-side fill-status transition, and paginated search. It exists to own all order persistence and the tricky in-SQL update that recomputes remaining size, avg price, and status atomically. Without it orders couldn't be stored, queried, or transitioned correctly on partial fills.

key_pieces.name: orderRepository.SyncTradeMatchingResult
key_pieces.note: A single UPDATE that decrements size, recomputes avg dealt price, and derives FILLED/PARTIAL status server-side.

key_pieces.name: orderRepository.PaginationQuery
key_pieces.note: Dynamically builds filtered, paginated order search with a matching count query.

why: SQLite trade repository: batch-insert executed trades and read latest price, price-at-time, and volume-in-range used for market stats. It exists to persist the trade tape and serve the price/volume aggregates market data needs. Without it there'd be no trade history and no way to compute 24h change/volume.

key_pieces.name: tradeRepository.BatchInsert
key_pieces.note: Single multi-row insert of a match batch, keeping the settlement transaction fast.

why: SQLite user repository: fetch by id/username and insert/update password and VIP/fee tier. It exists to own all user-table SQL behind the repo interface. Without it registration, login lookups, and fee-tier reads would have no persistence.

Fetches a symbol's last price from the BTSE public market-summary API, falling back to 0.01 when not found. It exists to seed candle init prices and provide an external reference price at startup. Without it the aggregator would have no external anchor for a symbol's first bar. Note it duplicates the BTSE client also present in the AMM proxy.

key_pieces.name: GetIndexPrice
key_pieces.note: The startup index-price source; its 0.01 fallback is exactly the value db.go later purges from candles.

why: An effectively empty file containing only the package declaration. Its purpose is unclear beyond possibly reserving the file for future package-init code; it provides nothing today and removing it would have no functional effect.

Defines the common Scheduler interface (Start/Stop/Name/RunTimes) that every background job implements. It exists so main.go and the reporter can treat all jobs uniformly. Without it the reporter and startup couldn't iterate jobs polymorphically.

key_pieces.name: Scheduler
key_pieces.note: The uniform contract that lets diverse jobs be started and reported on generically.

why: The periodic liquidity job: every interval it drives the provide-liquidity strategy across all markets using the internal AMM account and per-market quote caps. It exists to continuously refresh market-making quotes so books stay liquid. Without it the AMM would run once or never and books would go stale/empty. Its Stop is an unimplemented panic, so it's start-only today.

key_pieces.name: LQDTScheduler.Start
key_pieces.note: Ticks the market-maker across every market each interval — the engine keeping demo liquidity fresh.

key_pieces.name: NewLQDTScheduler
key_pieces.note: Fatally requires the reserved AMM account to exist at boot, coupling this job to the seed data.

why: Periodically recomputes each market's ticker (price/change/volume) and writes it to the shared cache, with run-count tracking. It exists to keep the cheap cached market data reads fresh without computing on every request. Without it the /markets endpoints would serve stale or empty data.

key_pieces.name: MarketDataScheduler.updateMarketData
key_pieces.note: The loop that recalculates and caches every market's ticker each tick.

why: A fast (300ms) job that refreshes each book's top-20 snapshot from the live engine. It exists so snapshot reads (REST and WS) hit a precomputed structure instead of locking and walking the live book per request. Without it depth reads would contend with matching and be slower.

key_pieces.name: orderBookSnapshotScheduler.Start
key_pieces.note: Periodically calls RefreshSnapshot per market, decoupling read load from the matching hot path.

why: Collects each scheduler's name and run-count into report structs for the metrics service. It exists to bridge scheduler internals to Prometheus without the metrics package depending on each job. Without it scheduler execution counts couldn't be exported.

The push engine behind live WebSocket updates: a 1s loop (all channels + sim tick) and a 400ms fast loop (order book only) that collect data per active subscription and broadcast it, including per-user private data resolved from the token. It exists to turn the pull-based services into real-time pushes at appropriate rates. Without it subscribed clients would never receive live candles, books, tickers, or account updates.

key_pieces.name: WSDataFeederJob.collectAndSend
key_pieces.note: Per-channel dispatch that assembles the right payload, including auth-gated USER_DATA via the credential cache.

key_pieces.name: WSDataFeederJob.Start
key_pieces.note: Two-rate design — fast 400ms order book, 1s for everything else — to balance freshness against send back-pressure.

why: Holds the Gin middleware that wraps every request: CORS (so the browser front-end may call the API), a central error handler that maps c.Errors to coded JSON, the auth middleware that resolves the Authorization token to a user via the credential cache, an admin-token gate, and a rate-limit stub. It exists so cross-cutting concerns live in one place instead of being copied into every handler. Without it endpoints would each re-implement auth/CORS/error shaping and the private/admin boundary would be unreliable.

key_pieces.name: AuthMiddleware
key_pieces.note: Resolves the (optionally Bearer-prefixed) token to a user and stashes user/userId/username in context, or aborts 401 — the gate every private route sits behind.

key_pieces.name: AdminMiddleware
key_pieces.note: Currently a demo-grade check against a hardcoded Admin-Token: frizo (marked TODO) — the privileged gate for reset/settlement.

key_pieces.name: ErrorHandler
key_pieces.note: Turns bind/public/system errors into the uniform coded envelope so failures look consistent to clients.
